✈️ travel memoir · israel

My First 24 Hours
in Israel

one wedding, a lot of strangers, and something that quietly rearranged everything ✨

the wedding dinner 🌿
the ceremony 🤍
golden hour 🌤
🛂

Arrival

I wasn't prepared for how intense airport security would feel.

At check-in, I was questioned for what felt like a very long time:

  • How do you know your friend?
  • Why are you going to Israel?
  • Where is the wedding?
  • Do you have other friends there?
  • How many?
  • What are their names?
  • Are you visiting surrounding countries?
  • And again: how do you know your friend?

The questions themselves weren't shocking.
It was the feeling underneath them.

For the first time in my life, I understood — physically — what it means to be treated as suspicious before you've done anything at all.

As a small Asian woman, I usually move through airports with others treating me graciously. Cooperative traveler. Low risk. Easy category.

Once, I accidentally sneaked a box of razors into my carry-on. It triggered an inspection, and the TSA guard took one look at me, joked about how there must be something really dangerous inside, and missed the razors completely.

I realized I had never really experienced what many Black and Latino travelers probably recognize immediately: that quiet erosion of dignity that comes from being examined instead of welcomed.

✦
🧳

Secondary Screening

Then there was a second stage.

A blonde woman with a thick Russian accent motioned for me to bring my bags over. Without much explanation, she began unpacking everything:

  • Laptop out and opened
  • Reading material inspected
  • Chocolate pulled from deep pockets
  • Every pouch opened

My backpack was emptied piece by piece onto a metal table.

She flipped slowly through my New Yorker, page by page. Suddenly even what I read felt like something requiring justification.

On paper, it was just logistics. Airports search bags all the time.

But this time felt violating.

My Chanel bag turned inside out. My carefully stacked lipsticks separated and rolled loose across the table — small objects from my private life rearranged by someone else's hands.

When she finished, she nodded and told me I could repack my things.

And then suddenly:

Normal life again.
✦
🛫

Transit

A five-hour flight.

🫒 Surprisingly excellent airplane snacks — olive oil crackers that tasted almost homemade, and a coconut sweet I immediately wished I'd taken three of.

Aviation emotional whiplash: existential scrutiny followed by juice boxes.

Arrival security in Tel Aviv felt almost relaxed by comparison. I downloaded Gett, bought a SIM card at the airport like I've now learned to do almost automatically, and slipped back into my traveling self:

  • competent
  • efficient
  • adaptable

There's comfort in knowing you can land almost anywhere and figure things out within an hour.

By the time I reached the hotel, it was nearly 4 a.m.

Small boutique room. Quiet street. The particular exhaustion where your body no longer understands geography.

A few hours later, still half-awake, I left for the wedding.

✦
💍

The Wedding

What surprised me most was that I knew no one — and somehow that made it better.

Usually weddings where you don't know anyone feel isolating. Everyone already belongs somewhere. Conversations close around you before you arrive.

This time, I sat beside a girl on a couch and we just started talking. She admitted she barely knew anyone either. Within minutes she introduced me to her friends, and suddenly I had:

  • a table
  • names
  • continuity

People kept checking on me.

Omer asked if I wanted a drink every thirty minutes, like it was his assigned responsibility. At some point we were feeding the bride and groom shots while laughing too loudly.

Strangers behaving like cousins.

✦
💬

Conversations

I noticed quickly that conversations felt unusually alive.

People listened fully. Humor was darker and warmer.

At dinner, I sat beside a married man who spoke with total engagement — not flirtatious or guarded, just curious. He explained he knew Yair through military service and still volunteered weekends in a technology unit.

He casually described a time Israeli intelligence identified missiles being sent toward Tel Aviv and predicted the exact block they would hit.

I remember leaning forward, waiting for the tragic ending.

"And then?" I asked.

He looked almost confused by my tension.

"Oh — people get alerts on their phones. They go to shelters. Usually the missiles are intercepted."

Usually.

And then the conversation moved on.

✦
🌐

Living With Risk

In many countries, danger feels abstract.
War exists somewhere else. Disaster is something you watch on the news.

In Israel, things felt different:

  • Missile alerts are real systems people know personally
  • Almost everyone has military experience or knows someone currently serving
  • Shelters are built into homes, schools, and hotels

But daily life does not pause.

At the wedding, people were still:

  • drinking and laughing
  • celebrating love
  • dancing wildly
Joy existed — amplified — alongside threat.
✦
🧥

Small Acts

Later, while dancing, Yair introduced me to his childhood friend Alex. I could feel the matchmaking energy immediately. I wasn't romantically interested, but hours later he noticed I was freezing outside.

He disappeared for a long time and returned with his jacket from the car.

Such a small act — but it struck me how quickly care was extended.

By the end of the night, something unfamiliar had happened.

For the first time at a wedding full of strangers, I didn't feel like an observer of someone else's community.

I felt absorbed into one.
✦
🌙

After

What stayed with me wasn't any single conversation.

It was the atmosphere.

People seemed able to be heavy — and also light — because you cannot survive being heavy every day.

Strangely, it felt familiar. Almost Asian.

The emotional attentiveness I grew up with. My mother's quiet awareness that stability is never guaranteed. The way proximity to geopolitical conflict sharpens care for one another.

Everyone felt more alive, as if they knew what mattered because they could lose it at any time.

That first day quietly rearranged how I thought about life.

I still feel a pull to return.

Life there feels closer to the surface. 🌿

✈️

written somewhere between two worlds

israel, day one ♡
